system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

Conversation started: Sunday, April 12, 2026 12:58 AM
Model: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
Provider: openrouter

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Please do not use markdown as it does not render. You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.
Important: cloud sandboxes may be cleaned up, idled out, or recreated between turns. Persistent filesystem means files can resume later; it does NOT guarantee a continuously running machine or surviving background processes. Use terminal sandboxes for task work, not durable hosting.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

check_interval.description: Seconds between automatic status checks for background processes (gateway/messaging only, minimum 30). When set, I'll proactively report progress.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified when the process finishes — no polling needed. Use this for tasks that take a while (tests, builds, deployments) so you can keep working on other things in the meantime.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/AGENTS.md]
# Law Firm Wiki — Compiler Schema

> This file is the specification for the LLM that compiles activity logs
> into structured knowledge articles. Read it in full before any compile
> or query operation.

## What You Are

You are a knowledge compiler for a personal injury law firm. Your job is
to read raw case activity logs and extract reusable institutional knowledge
— the process, pitfalls, patterns, and best practices that make a PI
practice work.

You are NOT creating a case database. You are building a **wiki** — a
structured collection of concept articles about HOW PI cases work, derived
from evidence in real cases.

## The Three Operations

### 1. COMPILE (ingest raw → update wiki)

Read activity logs and case files. For each meaningful pattern you identify:

1. Check `index.md` — does a concept article already exist?
2. If yes → UPDATE it: add new evidence, adjust confidence, update Related
3. If no → CREATE it: full article per the Article Schema below
4. If two concepts interact → create/update a Connection article
5. Update `index.md` with any new or modified articles
6. Append a summary to `log.md`

**What to extract:**
- Process patterns (what happens and in what order)
- Decision points (why certain choices were made)
- Problems and resolutions (what went wrong, how it was fixed)
- Timeline patterns (how long things actually take)
- Communication patterns (what works with adjusters, clients, providers)
- Edge cases (unusual situations and how they were handled)
- Anti-patterns (things that caused problems)

**What to IGNORE:**
- Individual client PII (names, SSN, DOB, addresses, phone numbers)
- Specific dollar amounts tied to identifiable cases
- Attorney-client privileged communications
- Raw email addresses and phone numbers
- Replace specifics with anonymized references: "Case A", "Case B", or
  descriptive patterns: "in cases involving [treatment gap > 30 days]"

**Confidence scoring:**
- high: Pattern observed in 5+ cases with consistent outcomes
- medium: Pattern observed in 2-4 cases
- low: Single case observation, or conflicting evidence

### 2. QUERY (ask questions → synthesize answers)

1. Read `index.md` to identify 3-10 relevant concept articles
2. Read those articles in full
3. Synthesize an answer with [[wikilink]] citations
4. If the answer reveals a gap → note it in the answer
5. Optionally file the Q&A back into `wiki/qa/`

### 3. LINT (health check)

Run these checks and write results to `wiki/reports/`:
- **Broken wikilinks**: Links that point to non-existent articles
- **Orphan articles**: Concepts with zero inbound links
- **Phase coverage**: Which PHASE_DAG phases have sparse knowledge
- **Stale articles**: Source data changed since last compile
- **Contradictions**: Articles that contain conflicting guidance

## Article Schema

### Concept Article (wiki/concepts/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Human-Readable Title
slug: kebab-case-slug
phase: [phase_0_onboarding]    # which PHASE_DAG phases this applies to
category: one-of-below
tags: [tag1, tag2, tag3]
confidence: high               # high/medium/low
evidence_count: 5              # number of cases with supporting evidence
created: YYYY-MM-DD
updated: YYYY-MM-DD
related:                       # other concept slugs
  - related-concept-one
  - related-concept-two
---

# Title

> One-paragraph summary (2-3 sentences max)

## Pattern

Detailed explanation of the pattern, process, or concept.

## Evidence

Anonymized observations from source cases:
- "In N cases where [condition], [outcome]"
- "When [action was taken], [result]"

## Agent Guidance

Actionable instructions for paralegal agents:
1. Step one
2. Step two
3. Step three

## Pitfalls

- Common mistake one
- Common mistake two

## Related

- [[related-concept-one]] — how it connects
- [[related-concept-two]] — how it connects
```

### Categories

- `process`: Standard workflows and procedures
- `adjuster-tactics`: Insurance company behavior patterns
- `edge-cases`: Unusual situations requiring special handling
- `phase-knowledge`: Phase-specific best practices
- `communication`: Client/adjuster/provider communication patterns
- `financial`: Billing, liens, settlements, funding
- `medical`: Treatment patterns, records, chronologies
- `legal`: Litigation, discovery, depositions, trial
- `tools`: Software, systems, and process tools

### Connection Article (wiki/connections/*.md)

Same schema but `category: connection` and the body explains how
two or more concepts interact. Example:

```
treatment-gaps-affect-demand-value.md
→ Links: treatment-gap-defense + demand-timing-strategy
→ Insight: Cases with documented gap explanations recovered 80% of
  expected value vs 50% for unexplained gaps
```

### Q&A Article (wiki/qa/*.md)

```yaml
---
title: Question text
slug: question-slug
date: YYYY-MM-DD
sources:                       # concept articles consulted
  - concept-slug-one
  - concept-slug-two
---

# Question

[Full question text]

## Answer

[Synthesized answer with [[wikilink]] citations]

## Gaps Identified

[Any knowledge gaps the question revealed]
```

## Index Schema (wiki/index.md)

The master catalog. This is the LLM's entry point for all queries.

```markdown
# Law Firm Wiki — Index

Last compiled: YYYY-MM-DD | Articles: N | Connections: N

## By Phase

### Phase 0: Onboarding
| Article | Category | Confidence | Evidence |
|---------|----------|------------|----------|
| [[concepts/onboarding-pitfalls]] | phase-knowledge | high | 15 |

### Phase 1: File Setup
...

## By Category

### Adjuster Tactics
| Article | Phase | Confidence |
|---------|-------|------------|
| [[concepts/treatment-gap-defense]] | 2,3 | high |
...

## Recent Updates

| Date | Article | Action |
|------|---------|--------|
| 2026-04-12 | treatment-gap-defense | created |
```

## Activity Log Format Reference

Activity logs live at `cases/<slug>/Activity Log/*.md` and have this structure:

```yaml
---
schema_version: 2
case_id: uuid
date: "YYYY-MM-DD"
time: "HH:MM:SS"         # optional
category: legal|correspondence|imported|financial|medical|task
subcategory: email|filevine_negotiation|outbound_call|...
---

# Category — Date

**Case:** [[cases/slug/slug|Client Name]]
**Source:** description

[Free-form content: emails, notes, call summaries, task descriptions]
```

### Subcategory Distribution (21K+ logs)
- email: 3,078
- filevine_negotiation: 1,620
- outbound_call: 890
- missed_call: 780
- filevine_treatment: 717
- filevine_demandinprogress: 645
- demand: 534
- filevine_filesetup: 369
- phase_change: 254
- filevine_uim: 179
- inbound_call: 137
- esignature: 78
- call_summary: 10

## PHASE_DAG Reference

The case lifecycle has 8 phases:
- Phase 0: Onboarding (contract, HIPAA, intake)
- Phase 1: File Setup (insurance claims, provider setup)
- Phase 2: Treatment (monitoring, records collection)
- Phase 3: Demand (drafting, review, sending)
- Phase 4: Negotiation (offers, counters, strategy)
- Phase 5: Settlement (agreement, disbursement)
- Phase 6: Lien Resolution (PIP waterfall, lien negotiation)
- Phase 7: Litigation (complaint, discovery, trial)
- Phase 8: Closed

## Rules

1. NEVER include identifiable client information in wiki articles
2. Anonymize all case references (use "Case A", "Case B", or descriptive patterns)
3. Every article must have at least one [[wikilink]] to a related concept
4. Update index.md whenever you create or modify an article
5. Append to log.md whenever you compile
6. Confidence scores must reflect actual evidence count
7. When in doubt about a pattern's validity, use confidence: low
8. Prefer specific actionable guidance over vague advice
9. Group knowledge by PHASE_DAG phase when possible
10. Flag contradictions explicitly — don't silently resolve them

[Subdirectory context discovered: /opt/data/FirmVault/CLAUDE.md]
# FirmVault — Agent Orientation

> **Read this in full at the start of every session.** It names the thesis, the non-negotiable rules, and where to find the authoritative docs. Short deliberately — use the imports below for detail.

## What this repo is

FirmVault is the **development environment for an AI paralegal backend** intended to eventually be sold to other personal-injury law firms. This specific repo is Whaley Law Firm's own instance; the architecture we prove here becomes the product.

Two parallel product tracks exist:

1. **This repo** — the GitHub-native paralegal backend: markdown vault, `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, `SKILL.md` library, runtime (materializer + worker), intake pipeline.
2. **Separate Huly fork** (not in this repo) — the lawyer-facing UI. This repo's "UI" is for operators, not lawyers.

## The thesis

**"The firm as a code repo."** Law firm work is repeatable tasks + templates + rules, which is exactly what code is. Put the firm's work into primitives agents already excel at (issues, branches, PRs, diffs) and you get a much better AI experience than any traditional PMS bolt-on.

## Core architectural principles

1. **The vault is a shadow, not the source of truth.** Real files (signed PDFs, audio, emails, faxes) live on the firm's own storage (their server, Dropbox, Box, Drive). The markdown in `cases/<slug>/` is a PHI-masked projection of those files.
2. **Intake is a pipeline, not an agent**: document arrives → real file placed on user's storage → converted to markdown → PHI stripped → opens a PR against this repo. The PR is the first human-in-the-loop gate.
3. **Git is the deliberate interface.** Every agent action is a commit or PR. Free audit trail, free approval gates, trivial rollback. Agents *never* touch real files — only their masked shadows.
4. **Markdown, not a graph DB.** We tried FalkorDB and abandoned it. LLM agents are empirically more reliable on plain text + grep than on Cypher queries. This is a deliberate choice, not laziness.
5. **PHI never lives in the vault.** Placeholders like `{{client_ssn}}` and `{{dob}}` reference secrets held in the user's own infrastructure. At render time, deterministic code (not the model) substitutes real values. The model never sees raw PII.
6. **The vault is the only state store for this layer.** No external database. No per-case JSON files. No FalkorDB. No `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`. See `@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md` for the authoritative mapping.

## Do NOT do these things

These are actively deprecated. If you see them in old code or docs, treat them as bugs.

- **Do not reference FalkorDB, Cypher, `graph_client`, `graph_manager`, or `run_cypher_query`.** All removed. Everything reads the vault.
- **Do not reference per-case JSON files** (`overview.json`, `contacts.json`, `insurance.json`, `liens.json`, `expenses.json`, `medical_providers.json`, `case_state.json`). Deleted.
- **Do not reference `${ROSCOE_ROOT}`** or any absolute-path scaffolding. Everything is repo-relative.
- **Do not edit content between `<!-- roscoe-medical-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-medical-end -->` or `<!-- roscoe-insurance-start -->` / `<!-- roscoe-insurance-end -->` markers.** Those blocks are owned by the import scripts and will be overwritten on next sync.
- **Do not invent new vault paths.** If the thing you want to write doesn't have a home in `DATA_CONTRACT.md`, update the contract first (same commit), then write. Drift between contract and reality is the bug.
- **Do not create top-level dashboards, reports, or state files outside the vault layout**. Operator views read the vault via GitHub API or git; they don't create parallel state.

## Where the authoritative docs live

Import these at launch — they are the contract, not me paraphrasing:

@DESIGN.md

@skills.tools.workflows/DATA_CONTRACT.md

@MEMORY.md

Also non-imported but worth knowing:

- `skills.tools.workflows/workflows/PHASE_DAG.yaml` — machine-readable phase definitions, landmarks, and predicate vocabulary. The materializer reads this.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/README.md` — how the materializer and worker agents drive the task queue.
- `skills.tools.workflows/runtime/task_schema.md` — the contract for task issues (labels, frontmatter, lifecycle).
- `Templates/INDEX.md` — the firm template library (73 slugified templates grouped by category).
- `skills.tools.workflows/Skills/*/SKILL.md` — 42 modernized skills in Anthropic Agent Skills format. Exemplars: `lien-management`, `deposition-prep`, `deposition-strategy`, `pip-application`.

## Slug rules (critical for wikilinks)

From `DATA_CONTRACT.md` §4:

- Lowercase
- Apostrophes and curly quotes **stripped** (no replacement char): `K'daya` → `kdaya`
- `&` → `and`
- All other non-alphanumeric runs collapsed to a single hyphen
- Leading/trailing hyphens stripped

Mismatched slugs break wikilinks across the vault. Slug before you write.

## Runtime model (what's being built)

The system is being turned from passive documentation into an active work-driving loop:

```
Vault (cases/*) ← materializer (cron) → GitHub Issues ← worker (event) → Vault
```

- **Materializer** walks open cases, reads each case's `landmarks:` frontmatter + `PHASE_DAG.yaml`, opens a GitHub Issue for every unsatisfied landmark whose task template preconditions are met. Already prototyped as a prompt in `runtime/materializer_prompt.md`.
- **Worker** is triggered by the `status:ready` label on an issue, claims it, loads the named skill, runs it, commits vault changes that reference the task_id, and flips the issue status. Prototyped in `runtime/worker_prompt.md`.
- **Source of truth** for whether a landmark is satisfied is `case.frontmatter.landmarks.<id>` — the materializer checks this first and only falls back to evaluating a predicate if the key is missing.

## Working branch

All in-progress work lives on `claude/import-cases-from-drive-xiQgL`. Do not push directly to `main` without an explicit request.

## When in doubt

- Read `DATA_CONTRACT.md` before writing to the vault.
- Read `PHASE_DAG.yaml` before touching workflow/landmark logic.
- Read `MEMORY.md` to find out what's in flight and what's pending.
- Check the last 3–5 commits on the working branch to see the most recent decisions.

<!--
MAINTAINER NOTES (stripped before injection; free context)

This file is intentionally short. If it starts approaching 200 lines, split
topic-specific rules into `.claude/rules/*.md` and reference them here.

Do not duplicate DATA_CONTRACT.md content — @-import it. The contract is
authoritative; this file is orientation.

MEMORY.md is a regular repo file we @-import here. It is NOT the same as
Claude Code's auto-memory (~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md) —
that's machine-local and managed by Claude Code itself. Our repo-level
MEMORY.md is the operational state tracker, committed to git.
-->

# Intake and Onboarding Workflow

> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.

# Case Phase Transitions

> PI cases follow a defined lifecycle: Lead -> Potential New Client -> File Setup -> Treatment -> Demand in Progress -> Negotiation -> Settlement. Each transition has specific prerequisites and is triggered by specific events. Understanding these transitions is critical for case management automation.

# Medical Records and Bills Collection

> Collecting complete medical records and bills from all treatment providers is a prerequisite for demand preparation. This process involves sending HIPAA authorizations, formal record/bill requests via fax and email, and persistent follow-up when providers are slow to respond. Gaps in records collection directly delay case resolution.

# Client Frustration Escalation Pattern

> PI clients exhibit a predictable pattern of escalating frustration when they feel the firm is not communicating with them. This pattern typically begins with polite check-ins, escalates to demands for immediate response, and can culminate in threats to change attorneys. Preventing this escalation through proactive communication is more effective than responding to it after it begins.

# Unreachable Client Patterns

> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.

# Underinsured Motorist (UIM) Claim Management

> When the at-fault driver's BI policy limits are insufficient to cover the plaintiff's damages, the plaintiff may pursue an Underinsured Motorist (UIM) claim against their own auto insurance carrier. UIM claims run as a parallel track to the BI claim and require distinct procedural steps including consent-to-settle notifications, separate complaint filing, and independent adjuster management — often with the same carrier that provides the client's PIP coverage.

# Statute of Limitations Monitoring

> The statute of limitations (SOL) is the absolute deadline for filing a lawsuit. In Kentucky PI cases, this is typically 2 years from the date of accident. Approaching the SOL without resolution requires escalation to the attorney for a decision: settle at current offer, file suit, or withdraw. SOL monitoring is automated in Filevine with countdown alerts.

# Health Insurance Lien Management

> When a PI client has health insurance that pays accident-related medical bills, the health insurer asserts a subrogation lien — a right to be reimbursed from any settlement. Managing these liens involves verifying their accuracy, removing unrelated charges, and ultimately negotiating a reduction. Liens directly reduce the client's net recovery.

# Demand Preparation Workflow

> The demand phase begins when the client's treatment is complete and all medical records/bills have been collected. The transition to "Demand in Progress" is a formal phase change that triggers a series of tasks: final records requests, records review for completeness and relatedness, lien identification, and ultimately the drafting and sending of the demand package to the insurance adjuster.

# Workers' Compensation Case Patterns

> Workers' compensation cases differ fundamentally from personal injury cases in their procedural framework, involved parties, and resolution mechanisms. WC cases are governed by state Department of Workers' Compensation (DWC) rules rather than civil court procedures, involve employer's WC insurance carriers rather than at-fault BI carriers, and resolve through administrative proceedings (Benefit Review Conferences) rather than jury trials.

# Workers' Compensation Claim Patterns

> Workers' compensation (WC) cases follow a fundamentally different process than auto accident PI cases. WC claims involve an employer's insurance carrier that must approve treatment, authorize specific providers, and pay Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits. Unlike BI claims where the firm collects records and sends a demand, WC cases require ongoing adjuster interaction to authorize each step of treatment.

# Client Communication Through Family Members

> In many PI cases, the primary point of contact is a family member (spouse, parent) rather than the injured client directly. This pattern creates both efficiencies (an engaged advocate) and risks (communication gaps, authorization requirements). Managing these relationships requires clear protocols around authorization, documentation, and expectation-setting.

# Expert Reports and Evaluations

> Expert reports — including Functional Capacity Evaluations (FCE), Independent Medical Examinations (IME), and vocational/economic analyses — are critical for establishing damages in PI cases, especially in litigation. They provide objective documentation of physical limitations, treatment outcomes, and economic impact of injuries. These evaluations require significant scheduling coordination and expense management.

# Dental Injury Case Patterns

> Auto accidents that cause dental and jaw injuries (TMJ, tooth fractures, jaw pain) follow a distinct treatment pattern compared to typical soft-tissue PI cases. Dental treatment involves multiple specialist providers (general dentist, endodontist, oral surgeon), staged procedures spanning 12+ months (root canals, extractions, implants, crowns), and creates unique challenges for case timeline management — particularly the risk of treatment extending near or beyond the SOL.

# Adjuster Persistent Unreturned Calls Pattern

> Insurance adjusters sometimes repeatedly call the firm, leaving multiple voicemails over weeks or months without getting a return call. This pattern can occur on both sides: adjusters trying to settle may be unable to reach the attorney, or the firm may deliberately deprioritize a carrier's calls while focused on other aspects of the case. Both directions carry risk.

# Case Manager Handoff Patterns

> PI cases frequently outlast the tenure of the case manager who initially worked them. When a case manager leaves or cases are reassigned, the incoming staff member must pick up an unfamiliar file, establish rapport with the client, and continue without losing momentum. These handoffs create a risk of dropped tasks, communication gaps, and client frustration.

# Medical Bills Going to Collections

> A recurring pain point in PI cases is medical bills going to collection agencies while the case is pending. This causes significant client anxiety about credit impact and creates urgency to resolve the case. The firm must manage both the practical issue (getting bills paid through PIP or other channels) and the emotional impact on the client.

# Multi-Carrier Multi-Defendant Case Management

> When an accident involves multiple at-fault parties (e.g., chain-reaction collisions), the plaintiff's firm must manage separate BI claims against each defendant's carrier simultaneously. Each carrier may have different adjusters, different liability postures, different policy limits, and different negotiation timelines. Coordination across these parallel tracks is critical to maximize recovery.

{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Intake and Onboarding Workflow
     3|slug: intake-and-onboarding-workflow
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup]
     5|category: process
     6|tags: [intake, onboarding, vinesign, welcome-call, insurance-setup, providers]
     7|confidence: high

     9|created: 2026-04-12

    12|  - case-phase-transitions
    13|  - client-communication-through-family
    14|  - file-transfer-from-prior-counsel
    15|---
    16|
    17|# Intake and Onboarding Workflow
    18|
    19|> The intake process converts a potential client into an active case. It involves collecting personal information, executing the retainer agreement, setting up insurance claims, identifying medical providers, and establishing communication channels. The workflow must be completed rapidly to preserve evidence and begin the claims process.
    20|
    21|## Pattern
    22|
    23|Standard intake workflow observed across cases:
    24|
    25|1. **Initial Contact**: Client (or family member) reaches out, usually by phone. Case manager gathers preliminary accident details, injuries, and insurance information.
    26|2. **Information Request**: Firm requests: Name, DOB, SSN, phone number, copy of health insurance card, names/addresses of hospital and PCP.
    27|3. **Contract Execution**: Intake documents sent via VineSign (electronic signature platform) for completion. Documents include fee agreement, HIPAA authorization, and other intake forms.
    28|4. **Welcome Call**: Once docs are signed, firm conducts a welcome call to confirm treatment providers and introduce the client to the process.
    29|5. **Phase Change**: Case moves from Lead/PNC to File Setup.
    30|6. **Insurance Setup**: Send Letters of Representation to both BI (at-fault) and PIP (client's own) carriers. Request acknowledgment.
    31|7. **Provider Setup**: Add all medical providers to the system. Send records/billing requests.
    32|8. **Phase Change**: Case moves from File Setup to Treatment.
    33|
    34|Timeline: This entire workflow typically spans 3-7 days from initial contact.
    35|
    36|## Evidence
    37|
    38|- In Case B, the initial contact came from the client's husband who provided detailed accident information, insurance claim numbers, and adjuster contacts. The firm requested personal info (DOB, SSN, etc.), sent VineSign intake docs, and received signed docs within 3 days.
    39|- In Case C, the intake and welcome call happened on day 1 (4/25/2024). Insurance claims were set up and LORs sent the same day. Records requests went out by 4/29.
    40|- In Case A, intake was complicated by the prior firm transfer — mom signed intake docs via VineSign, but the file transfer from the prior firm took over a week.
    41|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA), the BI adjuster proactively called the firm looking for the LOR the day after the accident report was filed. LORs were sent to both BI and PIP within 1 day. The provider discovery process was incremental — the client provided some providers on day 1, then additional providers were identified over subsequent calls (TN hospital, TX orthopedic, concussion specialist). This iterative provider discovery extended across weeks. A second accident (occurring one month after the first) was identified during intake, requiring separate claim setup for an NYC cab company (American Transit Insurance).
    42|- In Case F (WC/other case, DOI 12/2022), intake was handled through CaseOpp system. The CaseOpp used the wrong contract type (PI instead of WC), requiring a re-send of WC-specific intake forms. The client's VM was not set up, requiring callback. DocuSign intake docs were viewed multiple times before completion — client reported difficulty with a required field. Despite multiple views, completion took 7 days from first send. Provider identification required a client callback where treatment history was confirmed (ER, orthopedic surgeon, PT).
    43|- In Case G (MVA, DOI 4/2023), intake docs were sent via VineSign on day 1. The welcome call revealed the client had a companion case (daughter) — her file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. Multiple attempts to reach the client failed (VM full), with the firm pivoting to SMS. SSN and health insurance card were not obtained during initial intake and required 3+ follow-up attempts over 2 weeks. The companion case pattern added complexity to intake.
    44|- In Case H (MVA, DOI 12/2022), intake came via a witness/referral (Robert Grant). The firm was unable to reach the client directly — both phone numbers resulted in no answer and no VM. The police report number was obtained from the witness. Eventually the firm reached the client through the witness. This pattern shows that difficult-to-reach clients require alternative contact strategies from the start.
    45|- In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.
    46|
    47|## Agent Guidance
    48|
    49|1. Request all necessary information in the first communication (don't spread it across multiple contacts)
    50|2. Send VineSign intake docs within 24 hours of initial contact
    51|3. If docs are not signed within 48 hours, follow up
    52|4. Conduct the welcome call within 24 hours of signed docs
    53|5. Set up insurance claims (LORs to BI and PIP) within 48 hours of signed docs
    54|6. Identify and add all medical providers within the first week
    55|7. Log the phase change from Lead to File Setup once docs are signed
    56|8. For referral cases (husband calling for wife, etc.), get the actual client to sign the docs
    57|
    58|## Pitfalls
    59|
    60|- Slow VineSign turnaround — follow up if not signed within 48 hours
    61|- Playing phone tag with the client for days during intake (use text/email as backup)
    62|- Not capturing all insurance information (both BI and PIP carriers) in the initial contact
    63|- Not asking about all medical providers during the welcome call
    64|- Not sending LORs promptly (this delays adjuster acknowledgment and can delay everything downstream)
    65|
    66|## Related
    67|
    68|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — intake triggers the first phase transitions
    69|- [[client-communication-through-family]] — intake often involves a family member
    70|- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — transfer cases have a modified intake workflow
    71|", "total_lines": 70, "file_size": 6310, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/intake-and-onboarding-workflow.md", "old_string": "- In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.", "new_string": "- In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.
- In Case J (MVA, DOI 1/2023, UM claim), intake triggered immediate SSN request and welcome call. Client was unreachable on first attempt; VM left. Contact via text/email was initiated same day. Two-week follow-up cycle started. Client eventually responded via text. Provider setup and insurance claims (BI + UM + PIP to same carrier) done within 4 days.
- In Case K (trucking MVA, DOI 2/2024), intake docs were scanned and forwarded by managing attorney. Client phone number was non-functional on first call attempt — staff pivoted to text. Client eventually reached via alternate number. No health insurance. File Setup phase change occurred same day as intake.
- In Case L (MVA, DOI 1/2023, multi-carrier), intake was rapid — records request to hospital sent via ChartSwap on day 1 after intake. LOR sent to both BI carriers (KFB and Progressive) within 24 hours. The client contacted firm about financial hardship (cancelled medical appointments, no transportation) within 6 days — highlighting how intake must set timeline expectations early.
- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2024), client was spoken to briefly — she was running errands. She preferred text messages. Firm immediately pivoted to text-based communication. Police report number and health insurance card were requested via text. Records requests sent same day.
- In Case N (WC+BI, DOI 6/2021, transferred from TN attorney), intake was emergency — SOL was the next day. Complaint was filed on the day of intake before any records or provider setup. This case skipped normal onboarding entirely; the firm went straight to litigation.
- In Case O (MVA, DOI 5/2024), intake docs sent via VineSign on day 1. Attorney flagged that 5 years of prior medical records were needed from all providers (not just accident records). This represents a higher documentation requirement for demand preparation."}

{"success": true, "diff": "--- a//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/intake-and-onboarding-workflow.md
+++ b//opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/intake-and-onboarding-workflow.md
@@ -43,6 +43,12 @@
 - In Case G (MVA, DOI 4/2023), intake docs were sent via VineSign on day 1. The welcome call revealed the client had a companion case (daughter) — her file had not been created yet, requiring coordination. Multiple attempts to reach the client failed (VM full), with the firm pivoting to SMS. SSN and health insurance card were not obtained during initial intake and required 3+ follow-up attempts over 2 weeks. The companion case pattern added complexity to intake.
 - In Case H (MVA, DOI 12/2022), intake came via a witness/referral (Robert Grant). The firm was unable to reach the client directly — both phone numbers resulted in no answer and no VM. The police report number was obtained from the witness. Eventually the firm reached the client through the witness. This pattern shows that difficult-to-reach clients require alternative contact strategies from the start.
 - In Case I (MVA, DOI 1/2024), intake moved from Potential New Client to File Setup to Treatment in a single day. Insurance companies were added, medical providers confirmed via text. The chiro office called proactively about PIP check routing. The client provided provider names via text ("Mary and Elizabeth, Starlite Medical Center"). Rapid same-day intake is possible when the client is responsive.
+- In Case J (MVA, DOI 1/2023, UM claim), intake triggered immediate SSN request and welcome call. Client was unreachable on first attempt; VM left. Contact via text/email was initiated same day. Two-week follow-up cycle started. Client eventually responded via text. Provider setup and insurance claims (BI + UM + PIP to same carrier) done within 4 days.
+- In Case K (trucking MVA, DOI 2/2024), intake docs were scanned and forwarded by managing attorney. Client phone number was non-functional on first call attempt — staff pivoted to text. Client eventually reached via alternate number. No health insurance. File Setup phase change occurred same day as intake.
+- In Case L (MVA, DOI 1/2023, multi-carrier), intake was rapid — records request to hospital sent via ChartSwap on day 1 after intake. LOR sent to both BI carriers (KFB and Progressive) within 24 hours. The client contacted firm about financial hardship (cancelled medical appointments, no transportation) within 6 days — highlighting how intake must set timeline expectations early.
+- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2024), client was spoken to briefly — she was running errands. She preferred text messages. Firm immediately pivoted to text-based communication. Police report number and health insurance card were requested via text. Records requests sent same day.
+- In Case N (WC+BI, DOI 6/2021, transferred from TN attorney), intake was emergency — SOL was the next day. Complaint was filed on the day of intake before any records or provider setup. This case skipped normal onboarding entirely; the firm went straight to litigation.
+- In Case O (MVA, DOI 5/2024), intake docs sent via VineSign on day 1. Attorney flagged that 5 years of prior medical records were needed from all providers (not just accident records). This represents a higher documentation requirement for demand preparation.
 
 ## Agent Guidance

messages.content:
{"content": "     1|---
     2|title: Unreachable Client Patterns
     3|slug: unreachable-client-patterns
     4|phase: [phase_0_onboarding, phase_1_file_setup, phase_2_treatment]
     5|category: communication
     6|tags: [client-communication, unreachable, voicemail-full, no-contact, phone-tag, text-fallback]
     7|confidence: medium
     8|evidence_count: 8
     9|created: 2026-04-12
    10|updated: 2026-04-12
    11|related:
    12|  - client-frustration-escalation
    13|  - client-expectations-management
    14|  - intake-and-onboarding-workflow
    15|  - case-decline-and-closing-workflow
    16|---
    17|
    18|# Unreachable Client Patterns
    19|
    20|> Some PI clients are persistently difficult to reach — their voicemail is full, they don't return calls, and emails go unanswered. This pattern is distinct from client frustration (where the client is actively trying to reach the firm). Unreachable clients create a bottleneck because the firm needs their input to proceed with intake, treatment confirmation, and case management. Persistent multi-channel contact attempts are required, sometimes over weeks.
    21|
    22|## Pattern
    23|
    24|### Common Unreachability Signals
    25|1. **Voicemail full**: The client's voicemail box is full, preventing the firm from leaving a message
    26|2. **No answer, no callback**: Multiple outbound calls with no answer and no return call
    27|3. **Phone number changes**: Client changes phone numbers without notifying the firm
    28|4. **Email unresponsive**: Welcome emails and information requests go unanswered
    29|
    30|### Multi-Channel Escalation
    31|The firm follows a predictable escalation pattern when a client is unreachable:
    32|1. **Day 1**: Outbound call → no answer / voicemail full
    33|2. **Day 1**: Text message sent as fallback
    34|3. **Day 2-3**: Second outbound call attempt + email
    35|4. **Week 2**: Follow-up task created ("Two Week follow up with client")
    36|5. **Week 3-4**: Additional call attempts, emails, texts
    37|6. **Ongoing**: "Still unreachable" logged in case notes
    38|7. **Eventually**: Client responds via a different channel or new phone number
    39|
    40|### Impact on Case Timeline
    41|- Intake tasks cannot be completed without client input (providers list, insurance card, SSN)
    42|- File Setup stalls — the case sits in File Setup for months instead of progressing
    43|- Medical records requests are delayed because providers aren't confirmed
    44|- The case may ultimately be declined partly due to the difficulty of working it without client cooperation
    45|
    46|### Client Eventually Resurfaces
    47|Unreachable clients often do eventually make contact, sometimes with a new phone number. When they do:
    48|- They expect the case to be progressing despite their absence
    49|- They may provide the requested information belatedly
    50|- The firm must quickly re-engage and catch up on delayed tasks
    51|
    52|## Evidence
    53|
    54|- In an auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the client was unreachable from day one of file setup. Multiple calls were made with the note "No answer, vm is full." Text messages were sent as fallback. The client eventually responded via text ("I will call you later, thanks") and then called back from a different number. The voicemail transcription showed they had also been trying to reach the firm: "We have missed each other for the last day or 2." Despite repeated outreach attempts over weeks, the client remained difficult to reach throughout the case. By 2/2025, the note still read "Still unreachable." The client's phone number changed at least once during the case.
    55|- In the same case, even when the firm was trying to close/decline the case, a staff member noted they needed to speak with the client about the case update — and had to use a different phone number than the one on file.
    56|- In a PL case (DOI 9/2023) in active litigation, the client called the attorney repeatedly (20+ missed calls over 7 months), but the calls went unreturned for extended periods. The attorney then made periodic outbound calls. This is a reverse unreachability pattern — the firm is unreachable to the client rather than vice versa, likely due to attorney bandwidth in a litigation-heavy caseload.
    57|- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
    58|
    59|## Agent Guidance
    60|
    61|1. If the first call goes to voicemail and VM is full, immediately send a text message
    62|2. If no response to text, send an email the same day
    63|3. Set a 2-week follow-up task automatically on every case where the client has not responded
    64|4. Log EVERY contact attempt (call, text, email) with outcome (answered, VM full, no answer, etc.)
    65|5. After 3 failed contact attempts, escalate to the attorney with a note about unreachability
    66|6. Always ask the client for a secondary contact number and an emergency contact
    67|7. When the client does respond, immediately gather all needed information in that same conversation — you may not get another chance soon
    68|8. Record the client's updated phone number in the case system immediately when it changes
    69|9. Consider whether unreachability is a pattern that makes the case unworkable — flag for attorney review
    70|
    71|## Pitfalls
    72|
    73|- Only trying to call and not using text/email as fallback
    74|- Not logging failed contact attempts (creates the appearance of no effort)
    75|- Waiting weeks between contact attempts instead of escalating channels
    76|- Not asking for a secondary contact number at intake
    77|- Not updating the phone number in the system when the client calls from a new number
    78|- Assuming unreachability means the client is uninterested — they may be dealing with medical/life issues
    79|
    80|## Related
    81|
    82|- [[client-frustration-escalation]] — opposite pattern where the client is trying to reach the firm
    83|- [[client-expectations-management]] — unreachable clients may still have expectations about case progress
    84|- [[intake-and-onboarding-workflow]] — unreachability most critically impacts intake tasks
    85|- [[case-decline-and-closing-workflow]] — persistent unreachability may contribute to case decline decisions
    86|", "total_lines": 85, "file_size": 6045, "truncated": false, "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

{"path": "/opt/data/FirmVault/wiki/concepts/unreachable-client-patterns.md", "old_string": "- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.", "new_string": "- In an elderly MVA case (DOI 1/2022) in litigation, the client's voicemail was explicitly noted as "full" when the firm attempted to call with case updates. The client called back repeatedly but calls were not answered. This voicemail-full pattern prevents both parties from leaving messages, creating a complete communication breakdown.
- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client's phone number was "not in service" when the firm tried to reach her during intake. A text was sent and a second number was tried. The client was eventually reached via an alternate number. This was a pattern of phone service disruption rather than willful non-response.
- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client called the firm repeatedly but no one answered — calls were logged as "missed" to multiple staff members. The client also texted: "Is your office open today?" The firm's unreachability to the client (many missed inbound calls over months) is the reverse pattern, creating friction and frustration.
- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client was initially unreachable — "call went to VM - left msg." The client then used an alternate phone number for subsequent contact. New phone numbers should always be captured immediately.
- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 8/2024), the client preferred text over phone calls. When staff called, the client was unavailable ("on an errand"). Text became the primary channel. Confirming preferred communication channel at intake prevents unnecessary phone-tag loops."}

messages.content:
{"content": "    35|
    36|### Stage 3: Threats and Distrust
    37|- Client explicitly threatens to leave
    38|- "I am not settling for what you are"
    39|- "You keep in touch with me or nothing"
    40|- "If you will today, you just please call me back today and just give me something"
    41|- Client may call multiple staff members when the attorney is unavailable
    42|
    43|### Stage 4: Repeated Unanswered Attempts
    44|- Client calls are logged as "missed calls" repeatedly over weeks/months
    45|- Voicemail transcriptions become shorter and more resigned
    46|- Client may call different staff members (attorney, paralegal, receptionist)
    47|
    48|### Key Triggers
    49|- Long periods of no proactive contact from the firm
    50|- Settlement offer communicated without full explanation
    51|- Being told the case is "on settlement" without a timeline
    52|- Calls going unreturned or to voicemail
    53|
    54|## Evidence
    55|
    56|- In an auto accident case (negotiation phase), the client's frustration escalated over 12+ months:
    57|  - 6/2024: "Hey are you going to get back with me or do I need to take another route. Let me know today"
    58|  - 3/2025: Voicemail — "will you please get back in contact with me... you keep in touch with me or nothing... call me back today and just give me something. I told you I am not settling"
    59|  - 3/2025-3/2026: Multiple missed calls logged (3/21/2025 alone had 2 missed calls + 1 outbound call + texts)
    60|  - 8/2025: Multiple missed calls to different staff members
    61|  - 2/2026, 3/2026: Continued missed calls with empty voicemail transcriptions
    62|- In a premises liability case, the client texted the attorney directly: "Haven't heard anything from you or an update since court on Monday, and just wanted to reach out" — followed by the attorney providing a substantive update about next steps.
    63|- In Case H (MVA, air evac, 2.5+ year case), the client communicated through a go-between (Ann Dattillo). Client called for updates repeatedly — "said she had not heard from anyone recently." Client was confused about deposition process ("said that her attorney was to come out to her residence"). After attorney departure, client needed reassurance about new point of contact. The intermediary requested a copy of the deposition and case update. This shows Stage 2-3 frustration compounded by staff turnover.
    64|- In Case I (MVA, disputed liability, litigation), the client's calls went unreturned for extended periods — 4 missed calls logged between Feb-Mar 2026 with no callback documented. Client had previously been responsive and cooperative (provided provider info via text, completed deposition). The communication breakdown occurred post-deposition when the case was in a waiting period. This confirms the pattern that frustration peaks during litigation gaps.
    65|", "total_lines": 101, "file_size": 11537, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=65 to continue reading (showing 35-64 of 101 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

- In a UM/litigation MVA case (DOI 1/2023), a family member texted: "Hi, checking with you to see if there has been any settlement agreement yet." Two months later another text: "We haven't heard a single thing about [the] case. What's going on with it? It's been well long enough." Then: "Hello, we haven't heard anything at all about my case. After the deposition, you said things went well and we had to wait a couple weeks before an agreement/settlement." The post-deposition waiting period triggered escalating frustration from both the client and a family proxy.
- In a trucking MVA case (DOI 2/2024), the client called the firm repeatedly over Oct-Feb (10+ missed calls across months). Calls were to the managing attorney but never answered. The client texted "Good Afternoon Mr. [attorney]" and "Is your office open today" — showing politeness that may mask frustration. The reverse pattern (firm unreachable to client) is equally damaging.
- In a multi-carrier MVA case (DOI 1/2023), the client (who initially reported cancelled medical appointments and transportation hardship within days of intake) continued calling the firm repeatedly — 10+ missed calls logged over Jan-Mar 2026. The firm made periodic outbound calls, but the volume of unreturned client calls suggests significant frustration.
- In a WC+BI case (DOI 6/2021), the client called multiple times in Dec 2025 — 3 missed calls in a single day to different staff members (attorney, paralegal). The firm eventually made outbound calls. The "shotgun dialing" pattern (calling every staff member sequentially) is a Stage 3 indicator.
- In a treatment-phase MVA case (DOI 5/2024), the client had multiple missed calls across Oct-Dec 2025. The firm was in the records-gathering phase and had not yet sent the demand. SOL countdown warnings (90 days, 180 days) were firing while the client was unable to reach the firm for updates."}

{"content": "    30|2. File a lawsuit to preserve the claim (can still settle after filing)
    31|3. Obtain a tolling agreement from the defense
    32|
    33|## Evidence
    34|
    35|- In Case C, the SOL countdown appeared in the activity log: "30 day(s) to SOL in Case Summary (as of 3/24/2026)" with the actual SOL date of 4/23/2026 (exactly 2 years from the 4/23/2024 accident date). Prior alerts at 180 and 90 days were also logged and struck through as passed.
    36|- In Case E (auto accident, DOI 2/5/2023), the SOL countdown appeared in the activity log on 8/9/2024: "5 day(s) to SOL in Case Summary (as of 1/31/2025)." The SOL date of 2/5/2025 (exactly 2 years from the accident) was approaching while the case was in demand/negotiation phase. The BI adjuster proactively called around the demand preparation time to check on status — adjusters may be aware of SOL timing and use it in their strategy.
    37|- In Case D (chain-reaction MVA, DOI 11/08/2022), a complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court approximately 1 year after the accident (case number 23-CI-005509), well before the 2-year SOL. Filing suit preserved the claim and allowed litigation to proceed while negotiations with a second carrier continued. The pre-emptive filing illustrates the pattern of using litigation as leverage when initial negotiation offers are unreasonably low.
    38|- In Case F (auto accident, DOI 4/7/2023), a complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court (25-CI-000116) approximately 22 months after the accident. The case involved prolonged dental treatment that delayed the demand process, and the firm filed before the 2-year SOL expired.
    39|- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), a UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580) approximately 14 months after the accident. The UIM complaint was filed against the client's own insurer (Allstate) separately from the BI claim against the at-fault driver's insurer (State Farm). A courtesy copy was sent to the UIM adjuster.
    40|- In Case H (auto accident, DOI 4/16/2021), a complaint was filed in Jefferson Circuit Court (22-CI-002878) approximately 13 months after the accident, well before the 2-year SOL. The early filing was driven by coverage denial on the BI claim (insurer denied coverage due to vehicle theft claim), requiring litigation to force coverage resolution.
    41|- In a declined auto accident case (DOI 3/2024), the Filevine SOL countdown continued running even after the case was moved to "Closing" status. Alerts counted down from 180 days through negative days (-30 days past SOL), demonstrating that automated SOL alerts are not suppressed when a case is closed/declined. The SOL expired at 3/6/2026 (exactly 2 years from accident). This confirms that SOL monitoring is purely automated and does not account for case status — staff must manually recognize when alerts on closed cases can be ignored.
    42|
    43|## Agent Guidance
    44|
    45|1. Calculate the SOL date at intake and enter it in the case management system
    46|2. Verify the SOL calculation: in Kentucky, PI is 2 years from date of accident
    47|3. At 180 days out, flag the case to the attorney with current status
    48|4. At 90 days out, escalate: what is the plan for resolution?
    49|5. At 30 days out, the attorney must make a decision: settle, file suit, or obtain tolling agreement
    50|6. Never let a case approach SOL without attorney awareness
    51|7. For multi-state cases, verify the correct jurisdiction's SOL (it may differ)
    52|
    53|## Pitfalls
    54|
    55|- Miscalculating the SOL date (especially for out-of-state accidents)
    56|- Relying solely on automated alerts without manual verification
    57|- Allowing a case to stall in negotiation as SOL approaches
    58|- Not escalating to the attorney early enough to prepare a complaint if needed
    59|- Assuming the SOL can be extended without a formal agreement

messages.content:
{"content": "    30|
    31|### Parallel Management
    32|- The UIM adjuster is separate from the PIP adjuster at the same carrier
    33|- The UIM claim number may be the same as or different from the PIP claim number
    34|- The UIM complaint is filed in the appropriate court (may be different jurisdiction than BI)
    35|- UIM litigation follows standard discovery and deposition procedures
    36|- The UIM carrier files an answer to the complaint and may retain defense counsel
    37|
    38|### Litigation
    39|- UIM complaints are filed against the client's own insurer — an adversarial posture against the client's own carrier
    40|- A courtesy copy of the complaint should be sent to the UIM adjuster
    41|- Discovery includes the plaintiff's first set of interrogatories/production requests to the UIM carrier
    42|- UIM litigation can proceed independently from BI settlement
    43|
    44|## Evidence
    45|
    46|- In Case G (auto accident, DOI 7/30/2023), the client's extensive injuries (including $45,830+ hospital bills, physical therapy, chiropractic care, and surgery) exceeded the at-fault driver's BI policy limits (State Farm, claim #1754K417G). The client had UIM coverage through Allstate Insurance (claim #723447272). The UIM claim required separate management:
    47|  - PIP adjuster (Margaret Sharp) handled PIP claim through Allstate
    48|  - UIM adjuster (Nick Burchette) handled the UIM claim separately
    49|  - A UIM complaint was filed in Fayette Circuit Court (24-CI-03580, "McCay v. Allstate Property and Casualty Insurance")
    50|  - Allstate filed an answer to the complaint
    51|  - A consent-to-settle form was obtained from Allstate before the BI claim could proceed
    52|  - Discovery commenced with plaintiff's first set of discovery to Allstate
    53|  - The case involved $319.82 in court filing fees, plus expenses for EvenUP ($500) and Digital Owl ($495) for case evaluation
    54|  - The client also had a Humana health insurance lien ($11,809) and pre-settlement funding ($31,200 from Legal Funding Partners)
    55|  - Defense counsel (Jennifer Ann Peterson) was retained by Allstate
    56|  - A separate "waiving subrogation" document was filed
    57|- In an auto accident case (DOI 10/2023, KY), the client's injuries included treatment at 8 providers (ER, chiropractic at multiple locations, radiology). The at-fault driver's BI coverage (through a state mutual insurer) was insufficient, triggering a UM claim through the client's own national carrier. A separate UM claim number was assigned and a distinct adjuster handled it. PIP was also through the same national carrier with yet another adjuster (the PIP team). The case entered litigation with all three insurance tracks active. This confirms the three-adjuster pattern at a single carrier: BI adjuster at the at-fault party's carrier, PIP team adjuster at the client's carrier, and UM adjuster at the client's carrier — each requiring separate tracking.
    58|- In an auto accident case (DOI 9/2021, KY), the case involved a UIM claim alongside the BI claim. The UIM carrier (a national insurance company) was the same as the PIP carrier. The UIM claim had its own claim number (same as PIP) but required a dedicated adjuster. Multiple adjuster reassignments occurred on the UIM claim. The case entered litigation after negotiation produced inadequate offers. This confirms that UIM adjuster reassignment follows the same pattern as BI adjuster reassignment, with the "fresh eyes" effect potentially unlocking movement.
    59|- In Case Q (auto accident, VA veteran, DOI 5/2023), a three-carrier structure was observed: BI (Kentucky Farm Bureau), PIP (Everett Cash Mutual Insurance via a third-party administrator), and UIM (Erie Insurance Group). The UIM claim was managed by a dedicated adjuster at Erie. While the BI claim was in active litigation (Morgan County Circuit Court 24-CI-00065), the UIM claim remained on a parallel track with separate correspondence to the UIM adjuster. The case demonstrates that UIM can coexist with VA liens — the firm had to manage BI litigation, UIM parallel negotiation, PIP benefit tracking, AND a $86K+ VA lien simultaneously. The attorney was working on "distribution of funds" as of early 2025, indicating the BI component may have settled while UIM remained open. This confirms the pattern where UIM claims outlast BI resolution.
    60|
    61|## Agent Guidance
    62|
    63|1. At intake, identify the client's own auto insurance UIM coverage limits
    64|2. Compare the at-fault driver's BI limits to estimated damages — if damages likely exceed BI limits, flag for UIM
    65|3. Set up the UIM claim as a separate insurance claim record, even if it's the same carrier as PIP
    66|4. Track the UIM adjuster separately from the PIP adjuster
    67|5. Before accepting any BI settlement offer, obtain consent-to-settle from the UIM carrier
    68|6. Send a courtesy copy of the UIM complaint to the UIM adjuster when filed
    69|7. Track UIM litigation deadlines independently from BI claim deadlines
    70|8. When evaluating the total case value, consider BI limits + UIM limits as the recovery ceiling
    71|9. Budget for additional expenses: UIM litigation may require case evaluation tools (EvenUP, Digital Owl) and additional records requests
    72|
    73|## Pitfalls
    74|
    75|- Forgetting to check for UIM coverage during intake
    76|- Not obtaining consent-to-settle from the UIM carrier before accepting BI settlement (can waive UIM rights)
    77|- Confusing the PIP adjuster with the UIM adjuster at the same carrier
    78|- Not filing the UIM complaint timely (UIM has its own SOL considerations)
    79|- Not recognizing that UIM litigation is adversarial against the client's own carrier
    80|", "total_lines": 87, "file_size": 7523, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=80 to continue reading (showing 30-79 of 87 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}

# Child Support Enforcement Lien Patterns

> In some PI cases, a state child support enforcement agency asserts a lien against the client's settlement proceeds. Unlike medical or insurance liens, child support liens arise from pre-existing family court obligations and can represent extremely large amounts relative to the case value. These liens are legally distinct from subrogation liens and may not be negotiable in the same way.

## Pattern

When a PI client has outstanding child support obligations, the state child support enforcement agency may place a lien on any settlement proceeds. This lien:

1. **Is identified during file setup** when the firm discovers the client's lien obligations
2. **Can be very large** — in observed cases, the child support lien exceeded $46,000, potentially consuming a significant portion or even all of the net settlement
3. **Is a government enforcement lien** — unlike health insurance subrogation liens, these are not typically subject to the same negotiation or reduction strategies
4. **Creates a unique tension** — the client may receive minimal or no net recovery from their PI case after satisfying the lien, creating frustration and potentially reducing the client's motivation to cooperate with the case

### Lien Priority
Child support enforcement liens may have priority over other liens depending on jurisdiction. The interaction between a child support lien and medical/insurance liens creates a complex distribution calculation at settlement.

## Evidence

- In an MVA case (DOI 1/2023, KY) involving a UM claim, the state child support enforcement agency asserted a lien of $46,260. The client also had a medical provider lien of $2,235. The combined lien amount significantly exceeded what would typically be expected as client net recovery on a case with approximately $17K in medical treatment. The child support lien was listed as "amount_confirmed," suggesting the firm verified the lien amount. The case was in litigation against the client's own UM carrier, and the large child support lien would substantially reduce or eliminate the client's net recovery.

## Agent Guidance

1. At intake, ask whether the client has any outstanding child support obligations
2. If child support is identified, contact the enforcement agency early to confirm the lien amount
3. Factor the child support lien into the case evaluation — it may change whether the case is economically viable
4. Discuss the lien's impact on net recovery with the client early to manage expectations
5. Consult with the attorney about whether any portion of the settlement may be exempt from child support liens
6. Track the child support lien separately from medical liens in the case management system

## Pitfalls

- Not discovering the child support obligation until settlement, creating a surprise reduction
- Assuming child support liens are negotiable like medical subrogation liens
- Not managing client expectations about the lien's impact on their net recovery
- Failing to account for the lien when evaluating settlement offers

## Related

- [[lien-management-health-insurance]] — different lien type but similar distribution impact
- [[medicaid-lien-management]] — another government lien type
- [[va-lien-management]] — another government lien type with distinct rules
"}

title: SOL Emergency Filing Skips Normal Case Workflow
slug: sol-emergency-skips-normal-workflow

tags: [sol, emergency, litigation, intake, workflow-bypass, prior-counsel-referral]

  - litigation-process-patterns
  - workers-compensation-case-patterns
---

# SOL Emergency Filing Skips Normal Case Workflow

> When a case is referred to the firm with the statute of limitations expiring imminently (within 24-48 hours), the normal case workflow (onboarding -> file setup -> treatment -> demand -> negotiation) is entirely bypassed. The firm must draft and file a complaint on the same day as intake, moving the case directly from onboarding to litigation. This creates a case that enters the system "backwards" — litigation is underway before records, providers, or even basic client information has been fully gathered.

## Pattern

### How It Occurs
1. A referring attorney (often out-of-state) contacts the firm about a client whose SOL is about to expire
2. The prior attorney may have handled a related claim (e.g., workers' compensation) but not the third-party BI claim
3. The firm must file suit immediately — often the same day — to preserve the claim
4. A complaint is drafted and e-filed before any records review, demand preparation, or even a complete intake

### Consequences
- The case enters the system in Litigation phase with incomplete client information
- Records collection happens after litigation has begun, not before
- Discovery requests from the defense may arrive before the firm has its own records
- The case requires retroactive file setup — gathering records, bills, and provider information while simultaneously managing litigation deadlines
- Settlement conferences may occur before the firm has fully developed the case value

## Evidence

- In a WC+BI case (DOI 6/2021, KY), the firm received a referral from a Tennessee attorney who had resolved the workers' compensation portion but not the third-party BI claim. The managing attorney emailed: "New client must file suit by tomorrow." A complaint was drafted and e-filed in Henry Circuit Court the same day. The case moved directly to Litigation phase. Records collection began months later. An IME was scheduled over a year after filing. A telephonic settlement conference occurred approximately 2 years into litigation. The case illustrates how emergency filing creates a long retroactive development process — the firm spent years building the case that should have been built before filing.

## Agent Guidance

1. When a SOL-emergency referral arrives, the attorney must immediately verify: (a) the SOL date, (b) the correct jurisdiction, (c) the correct defendant names
2. File the complaint first — everything else can wait
3. After filing, immediately begin the retroactive intake: client contact, insurance claims, medical records requests
4. Flag the case internally as "filed pre-development" so all staff know records/demand were not done pre-filing
5. Prioritize records collection — defense counsel will request them in discovery

## Pitfalls

- Misspelling defendant names in the complaint (no time for research)
- Filing in the wrong jurisdiction
- Assuming the referring attorney's file is complete — it often lacks records relevant to the BI claim
- Not adjusting the case timeline expectations for staff (this case will develop slowly because everything is retroactive)

## Related

- [[statute-of-limitations-monitoring]] — the emergency that triggers this pattern
- [[case-phase-transitions]] — this pattern violates the normal phase sequence
- [[file-transfer-from-prior-counsel]] — the referral from prior counsel triggers this
- [[litigation-process-patterns]] — the case enters litigation without normal precursors
- [[workers-compensation-case-patterns]] — WC/BI dual-track cases are a common source of this pattern
"}

{"content": "    30|### Demand Preparation Sequence
    31|1. **Phase Change**: Status changed from "Treatment" to "Demand in Progress" with reason logged
    32|2. **Final Records Requests**: Any outstanding records/bills are requested or re-requested
    33|3. **Records Review**: Medical records are reviewed for:
    34|   - Relatedness to the accident (unrelated treatment must be separated)
    35|   - Completeness (all visits documented)
    36|   - Referrals to other providers discovered in records (new providers must be added)
    37|4. **Lien Identification**: Health insurance liens identified and lien amounts requested (e.g., from Rawlings/WellCare)
    38|5. **Demand Drafting**: Attorney prepares the demand package
    39|6. **Demand Sent**: Package sent to the BI adjuster; follow-up task created
    40|
    41|### Records Review Reveals New Providers
    42|A critical pattern: when records from one provider are received and reviewed, they may reference visits to other providers not previously on file. These must be added and their records requested before the demand can proceed.
    43|
    44|### Adjuster Proactive Contact During Demand Prep
    45|Insurance adjusters may proactively call to check on claim status during the demand preparation period, especially as the case approaches SOL. This should be documented and the adjuster given a general update without committing to specific timelines.
    46|
    47|## Evidence
    48|
    49|- In an auto accident case (DOI 2/2023), the client confirmed treatment was complete during a phone call on 3/11/2024 ("done treating with all medical providers listed on file. No more hospital visits."). Phase was changed to "Demand in Progress" that same day. Records review revealed a referral to White House Clinics and King's Eye Care not previously on file — both had to be added and records requested. Lien request was sent to The Rawlings Company (WellCare) during this phase. The BI adjuster (Clearcover) proactively called to follow up on claim status.
    50|- In the same case, records from providers were separated for relatedness (unrelated visits excluded) before demand preparation.
    51|- The demand was eventually sent, but the case then moved to "Settlement" and then back to "Negotiation" when the client refused the offer, indicating the demand-to-settlement pipeline is not always linear.
    52|- In Case M (MVA, DOI 8/2023), the demand preparation used EvenUP (a third-party AI demand tool) to generate the initial demand package. The attorney saved the EvenUP output to the file, then directed staff to edit it and send to both BI carriers. The demand was sent to both carriers on the same day (1/10/2024), with the case simultaneously transitioning from "Demand in Progress" to "Negotiation." The adjuster acknowledged receipt and promised evaluation "by the end of this week." The next day, policy limits (25/50) were tendered. This demonstrates the fastest observed demand-to-offer turnaround: 1 day from demand sent to limits tendered. EvenUP integration into demand preparation is a significant workflow accelerator.
    53|
    54|## Agent Guidance
    55|
    56|1. When a client reports treatment is complete, confirm via phone and document the call
    57|2. Trigger the phase change to "Demand in Progress" and log who authorized it
    58|3. Immediately create tasks for any outstanding records/bills requests
    59|4. When records arrive, review them for references to other providers — add any new providers found
    60|5. Separate records by relatedness to the accident
    61|6. Send final health insurance lien requests (to Rawlings, Optum, etc.) to get lien amounts
    62|7. Compile all records, bills, and lien information for the attorney's demand preparation
    63|8. When an adjuster calls proactively, document the contact and provide a general update
    64|9. After demand is sent, create a 30-day follow-up task for adjuster response
    65|
    66|## Pitfalls
    67|
    68|- Moving to demand before all providers are identified (records review may reveal new ones)
    69|- Not separating related vs. unrelated treatment in the medical records
    70|- Forgetting to request health insurance lien amounts before the demand
    71|- Not documenting the client's confirmation that treatment is complete
    72|- Failing to follow up with the adjuster after demand is sent
    73|- Assuming the demand-to-settlement path is linear (client may reject the offer)
    74|
    75|## Related
    76|
    77|- [[medical-records-collection]] — records must be complete before demand
    78|- [[case-phase-transitions]] — demand is a formal phase change with prerequisites
    79|- [[attorney-review-triggers]] — attorney reviews and approves the demand
    80|", "total_lines": 80, "file_size": 5622, "truncated": true, "hint": "Use offset=80 to continue reading (showing 30-79 of 80 lines)", "is_binary": false, "is_image": false}
